user_story: As a user reviewing transcripts, I want the system to use generic labels (e.g., 'Speaker 1', 'Speaker 2') instead of potentially incorrect specific names when speaker identification confidence is low or voices overlap, so that I can trust the accuracy of speaker attribution.
acceptance_criteria: The system accurately identifies segments with low speaker identification confidence or overlapping voices.
For such segments, the system automatically assigns generic labels (e.g., 'Speaker 1', 'Speaker 2') instead of named individuals.
The transition between named speakers and generic labels is smooth and logical within the transcript display.

user_story: As a user viewing long transcripts, I want the transcript content to load and render in chunks as I scroll, so that I can experience smooth performance and responsiveness without delays.
acceptance_criteria: The initial load of a long transcript displays only the visible portion of the transcript.
As the user scrolls down, subsequent chunks of the transcript are dynamically loaded and rendered.
Scrolling performance remains smooth and responsive even with very long transcripts (e.g., > 1 hour duration).
All core transcript features (e.g., search, playback synchronization) remain functional across loaded and not-yet-loaded chunks.

user_story: As a user interacting with multilingual content, I want the system to detect and switch to the correct language more quickly by detecting per phrase, so that I experience a fluid and accurate multilingual transcription.
acceptance_criteria: The system detects language changes within individual phrases or short sentences, rather than waiting for complete sentences.
Language switching occurs within a noticeably shorter duration (e.g., less than 1 second) after a new language phrase begins.
The transcribed output accurately reflects the language spoken for each phrase or segment.
The system does not 'stick' to English for an extended period when another language is predominantly spoken.
